speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 11 in the interview with Mr. Stefan Stok. At the end of the 10th roll, you were telling me about the liberation time that found you by the Koper's in the hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was still early, but actually it was still far off because I was talking about how the life at Koper's. And basically, what happened there is that we were very well informed because there was-- there were the underground newspapers, and that were, of course, German newspapers and the Polish-- official Polish newspaper. So we knew about the progress of the front and the fact that the Germans are-- at least in our eyes, we're certain that the Germans were losing the war, and that, particularly, the Russians are coming near. But we knew as well other thing because there was a book, or at least-- yes, a book published in probably Warsaw during the war called or written by somebody called [INAUDIBLE], which he described in the greatest detail everything that happened in Treblinka and how the people, Warsaw Jews, were killed, and all the details, including the gas chambers and so on and so forth. So we knew all that already. I think probably it was in 1944, because this chap escaped-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] should I look at--

speaker: interviewer
text: There is someone to answer.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It will take time. I better answer it I think. That's better. So we knew everything about what was happening this time in great detail. And we were staying at Koper's, although it was-- we knew that it was terribly dangerous. But we had no other-- there was no escape for us because we had no money anymore, and there was no other possibility. But actually, there were some people who left Koper on occasion. There was a lawyer there, Goldstein, who lived with us at Koper, as well. But on one occasion, he said Koper had belonged to the underground organization, and we gestapo arrested somebody in this underground organization. And we were told maybe it was not-- maybe it didn't happen-- that there was very likely possibility that the gestapo found in this place a paper with names and addresses of the members of the organization. And if that happened, then we were gone and finished. And Goldstein said then enough is enough, I am not stay here anymore. And he left. And we didn't leave because there was nowhere to leave. And stayed only in the flat, and somehow the gestapo never appeared. So we were extremely lucky. Maybe they never found the paper. On another occasion, there was-- they surrounded all of the area, and were-- got all of the people out and were making searches and selection. Those things happened in Polish area, as well. And were searching the houses. And we were all sitting on this occasion in the cellar and really not having much hope that we'll survive this time. And what happened, what was worse, was that there was this cellar. There was a hole to the outside, which appeared on the outside as a hole. It was kind of a long hole so that you couldn't look into the cellar, but there was a hole near the ground level. And because it was-- and because the Germans were around, I think my father pushed some rags or clothes into that hole so that if there was any sound, that they wouldn't hear from outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then the Germans were walking outside, and we heard them that they found those rags and they got their rags out. And they were talking to one another, what does it mean, and where are those regs from? But for some reason, they came to the conclusion that somebody-- that there was maybe-- that they were stolen goods who somebody just pushed quickly there to hide them. And they didn't go into the flat and they didn't search and they didn't find us, which was, you know-- it was all, as I said, a lottery ticket. It was pure luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: And luck and as well the fact that people at Koper's never even dreamt of-- of denouncing us-- on the contrary. And that Marta Koper was an extraordinary person who was looking after us with real devotion and with-- and naturally [INAUDIBLE] as if it was an obvious thing. Never being upset, always being very optimistic, and always getting us food, and always being friendly to us, although her life was as well very difficult. Because it was, after all, this small flat of two rooms and a kitchen, and we had-- on every bed there were two or three people sleeping. And there was no place simply to-- I slept with my father and sometimes I slept with my aunt and other people. Everybody had to share a bed almost, and it was altogether crowded out of every recognition. And during the day, as well, everybody was so crowded together. People were getting on each other's nerves. But somehow we managed to endure all that. And in 1944-- in August 1944, on the 1st of August, the Polish uprising started. The Polish uprising started mainly on the other bank of the Vistula, in the main part of Warsaw on the left bank of the Vistula. And then it went on for next two months. But on Praga, in our part of Warsaw east of the Vistula, also there was an uprising, but that one was repressed by the Germans. The Germans managed to cause the surrender of the uprising after only a few days. And then they practically emptied all of this part of Warsaw. People had to leave their houses, and they were evacuated. And anyway, the Russians were coming quite close. I think that by then, the Russian artillery could be already heard because they were coming quite close to Warsaw. In August they were-- they were coming close, and then the Polish uprising started because the Polish command thought that the Russians were there any day, practically. But the Russians then stopped east of Warsaw and didn't progress any further, whether it was on purpose or not. And they then stood quite close to Warsaw for about a month, and they didn't even come all the way to the Vistula. So we remained politically on the front line. For the next six weeks, maybe, we remained on the front line. And there-- were-- it was, as well, our-- the Kopers, our lent householders, they were not there as well because they had to evacuate-- to get evacuated. So they left probably in early August, and they went-- we knew where they went. They didn't go terribly far. They went to their little shed somewhere, about 15 or 20 kilometers from Warsaw. And we're left completely alone in this terrain. There was not any authority anymore, except there was the army there, and the army was the soldiers, so we were spending most of the time in the cellar. The soldiers were coming occasionally. And one of us, this German chap who could speak well German, he sometimes appeared upstairs and spoke to the soldiers, but the soldiers didn't-- obviously, they were noticed as they were not interested in exactly who it was that still was there. But we didn't have-- we didn't have-- there was nobody there except for the soldiers, and we were under-- that was all under artillery fire. The Germans were shooting from the west and the Russians were shooting from the east, so it was not exactly cozy. And there was nothing to eat, either, because the food was-- by then, any food that we had was finished, and there was no food to be had.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the only food that we could find was because there were [INAUDIBLE] gardens. There were some vegetables growing there and growing in the zoological garden, which was-- which was quite near, in fact, where there were no animals, but where it was all planted with things like potatoes and cabbage and beetroot and so on. So we had still some food. The food was-- in the night, some men were going out and finding some vegetables, and we were eating that, although there was very little of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on my birthday again, in the end of August, I think my father got some tomatoes for me, which was a great feast because that was so unbelievable that you could get some tomatoes. And we're getting quite hungry, but because there was no bread, there were just those vegetables. Potatoes-- there were few potatoes, so we were living on those, on some potatoes and on some other vegetables. And in this way-- and then in September, the front was coming. Came near it again. And on 14th of September, somebody woke me up early in the morning and we looked out of the door early in the morning, still hiding, and we saw that on the street, which was about a couple of hundred meters to the left-- to the right, sorry-- there were-- was-- German army was-- was going down. And they all were walking in-- not in any organized way, but they were walking in groups, but they were walking and walking and walking, and some cars and some armored cars. And they were-- and they walked and walked for a long time, and then they disappeared. And then there was peace and quiet and there was nobody there for a while. For an hour or two hours, there was nobody there. And we couldn't even believe that the Germans went. I mean, they went there, and we didn't know exactly where they were, but they went there. That was the way to the north. They must have gone to the bridge, I suppose. There were still some-- must have been some pontoon bridge, because the bridges, they sprang-- they exploded the big bridges on the Vistula. And few hours later, the Russians appeared. There were some Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Soldiers, yes. Soldiers appeared suddenly. We saw again soldiers, Russian soldiers, coming. And then we didn't dare to go out. And then the soldiers appeared all over the terrain. And some soldiers came to our door, and we-- my father could speak Russian so he spoke to them, but I think he didn't say exactly who we were. And they were friendly. They talked to us, but they had a job to do, so they went on and there was some shooting during that day-- not an awful lot, but we're so used to shooting. Shooting went all the time and artillery went all the time. And we didn't dare to leave the house on this day, although the Russians were already there. But we still didn't feel we were safe to leave the apartment, and we left the apartment only on the day after. And we were then suddenly free. And when the Germans disappeared on this-- on this day, on the 14th, I remember-- when we realized, because it took us a long time to realize that the Germans were not there anymore-- I went on my knees and I prayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: The second time.

speaker: interviewee
text: The second time. I prayed-- no, I was so-- I prayed a thanks prayer that I'm allowed to be alive, that I'm allowed to be-- because one was not allowed to be alive. One was condemned to death, practically, for years. For years, one was aware of that-- that any word, any step, anything, is-- is-- practically can kill you. So that was suddenly, one was allowed to live. One was-- one had suddenly a right to live as a human being. So that was something that was quite earth-shaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we left the [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] Koper's flat the day after. And I went with my father, and we spent still about a week or longer in Praga. And hello? And spent a week or longer in Praga, and then finally decided to-- my father decided, rather, to move after that more to the east, further from the front. And then we walked-- we spent some days in Praga, and then we walked across some 10 kilometers to [INAUDIBLE] to a little place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we moved from-- and that was still all a front line. There was artillery there everywhere and shooting. And then we went to the further away from Warsaw, and my father decided that he wants to go to Lublin where the first Polish government, the communist government, was. And we, somehow, in one way or another by walking and by horse cart and so on went to Lublin, and finally got to Lublin probably at the end of September.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you stayed in Lublin for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my father found that there was this Polish-- first Polish government, which was practically all Poles who were in Russia and the Soviet Union during the war. And so that was a communist government. And my father, since he was a lawyer and he found some people who knew in there, in the Department of Justice in this government, he found-- they offered him a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a lawyer?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a lawyer, yes. Well, working for the ministry, for the justice ministry. And he worked in Lublin in the next few months. And since we worked for the government, we got as well a room in an official apartment block belonging to the government, so we had a place to live. And suddenly life became relatively normal. I went to school and we had enough to eat, and we had-- and we had a room to live and everything. Of course, my mother was not there, and we didn't know what happened to my mother because she was on the other side of the Vistula and she was caught up in the uprising and could never then cross to the other side of the Vistula. And, in fact, my mother was totally separated from us until the late January '45, because only after another offensive, the Russians took the remaining part of Warsaw in January '45. And my mother then crossed over the Vistula, over the ice, I think, to the eastern part and went back to Koper's place and left a-- and left a letter for my father. And so we found out that she's alive, and she then came to Lublin, and we all were reunited--

speaker: interviewer
text: Became a normal family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Early-- early in 1945. Well, normal if one can say normal after all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: After all that, it's considered normal.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was relatively normal in view of what happened before. And then we moved back to Warsaw soon, a few months later in early spring, 1945. And because my parents had no place to live, I went to the children's home, which was kept by the Jewish committee in [POLISH], not far from Warsaw. And I stayed there for quite a few months until late in 1945. And then I moved again with my mother to [POLISH], and we lived there for another few years until my father managed to arrange for us a flat in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meanwhile he worked in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father worked in Warsaw and came to see us on weekends, and then we all moved back to Warsaw three years later in 1948, and lived in Warsaw until early in 1950 when we all went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then during this time, of course, you-- you made your school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went to school. Yes, I went normally to school. And the remaining part of my-- those of my uncles who survived the war, there was only one uncle, in fact, who survived the war, and there were a few aunts who survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it from your father or mother's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- from my father's side-- from my father's side nobody survived the war in Poland. Just one uncle, whom I talked about before was shot in 1943. And my father's sister, who ran with us from Warsaw in 1939 managed to get through Soviet Union to Palestine, to Israel, already in 1940, and she spent all the war in Tel Aviv. And so from my father's side, nobody really survived apart from us. But from my mother's side, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a few of them survived. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] One-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] three of my aunts. Three of my aunts and one-- but that was a big family-- and one uncle. And another aunt, who was the-- who was the doctor, who was shot, as I explained, in 1941. And another one survived in the Soviet Union. The painter survived in the Soviet Union, far away in Central Asia and came back to Poland in 1946. And another one left in France before the war and was taken to Auschwitz. That's it. And my cousins were with me in the ghetto, whom I talked about. Most of them didn't survive, as well. The cousin who saved us from the march to Treblinka-- he was only 18 and 19, and he killed himself in the ghetto. He committed suicide. And [POLISH] was my other cousin. Was caught and went to Majdanek and was killed there. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were one of the lucky ones.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very lucky. And the brothers of my mother-- one died in the beginning of the war and another one was-- and another who was killed in Trawniki in '43. And the other one was killed during the uprising when they discovered their hiding place. And another one, who [INAUDIBLE] was killed. Nobody knew that he was Jewish, but he was hit during the bombardment in Polish uprising, and he probably died in the hospital during uprising because we looked for him, one of my aunts and myself, in Warsaw in April '45 after the war. And we found as much that he was hit by that bomb and that they took him to the hospital, and then there were the other traces.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I would like to ask you about-- you and your family stayed in Poland about six years-- six-- five to six?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not six. We stayed until--

speaker: interviewer
text: Till '50.

speaker: interviewee
text: About five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: About five years--

speaker: interviewee
text: Five years after the war to-- to early '50, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other Jews that stayed and came back to Warsaw to live that you know about? Other Jews that came back to live in Warsaw like yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: But of course there were many.

speaker: interviewer
text: Many of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were many who-- there were some people who survived the war like us, and there were quite a lot who came from the Soviet Union who survived the war because-- what I said in the beginning was that lots of Jews who were deported in early-- in early years, in 1940, 1941, like my aunt Dora and my grandfather, to Soviet Union. They went to Siberia. And they had terrible conditions there, but most of them survived the war. And not my grandmother, because she died in Uzbekistan in Central Asia. But most of them did survive the war. And lots of them came back to Poland after the war. And not so many to Warsaw, maybe, but many came to Silesia, other parts of Poland. But most of them emigrated in the years after, but it was not always possible to emigrate. Emigrated means that that had to be with the agreement of the authorities. And that agreement was sometimes given and sometimes not, so there were periods when Jews were allowed to emigrate and there were long periods when they were not allowed to emigrate. They were allowed to emigrate immediately after the war. After the war, immediately, there was so much chaos that you didn't need any permission. You could leave Poland somehow without any permission. The borders were not formally fixed and so on, so people did leave. And then I think in '46 it was still possible to leave, and then it was not possible to leave for some time. And then it was possible to leave, I think, for people of-- young men of army age for Israel during the Independence War in Israel in '48, and then again in 1950 when we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so you were one of a group or a lot of people that immigrated this year, 1950, to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were lots of Jews who went to Israel in 1950. I don't know how many numbers, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but just-- was not only you. That's what I'm actually asking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no, we went-- there were trainloads. There were trainloads and boatloads and there must have been altogether thousands, but how many thousands, I'm not sure. But we went to quite officially, except that we had to give up our Polish nationality and give up most of our sayings and give up the furniture which we had. We had practically to go with few belongings, but we weren't quite officially with the train from Warsaw in early-- late January, '50 to Venice. And then in Venice we went on a boat, [ITALIAN], an Italian boat, and we went to Haifa. And we came to Haifa, so there was snow all over in Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Snow in Haifa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in February, 1950, there was a lot of snow, strangely enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and then when you arrived in Israel, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents, we all went to start to [INAUDIBLE] camp, which was not a very nice place. And we stayed there-- my parents stayed for a bit longer. I stayed only very briefly because I went to my aunt in Tel Aviv, the aunt who came from Warsaw in 1940. And she was well-established in Tel Aviv, so I stayed for a few weeks with her. Then I went back to my parents, and my parents were in some kind of a camp for [INAUDIBLE], which was--

speaker: interviewer
text: New immigrants.

speaker: interviewee
text: New immigrants, which was quite all right, but I was not enthusiastic about it. So because I was already, after the war, in Zionist movement in [HEBREW] I went to a kibbutz [HEBREW] to a group of youth who [HEBREW] and [HEBREW], not far--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the name of--

speaker: interviewee
text: --from [INAUDIBLE], and stayed there for another-- for two years, working and studying and so on, learning Hebrew, which I didn't know any to start. And then I left that place in 1952 and went back to school and studied for another year and did my exams and went to the army, basically.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you served in the Israeli army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Served in the Israeli for--

speaker: interviewer
text: And which year was it? Which year--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was from '53 to '55. And studies in Israel-- served in Israeli army. And then I made an entrance exams to Technion, to the institute of technology in Haifa, and got accepted for architecture and studied architecture for the next five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: You graduated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then it is more complicated because my parents went to Brazil, so I--

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't stay in Israel for long.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't stay in Israel for good, no. They went to Israel, which-- they went to Brazil because part of that family of ours who survived in Warsaw, they went to Brazil after the war. And my mother's sister, her sister that she was very fond of, lived in Rio de Janeiro, and she wanted to be near her. So she went to Brazil once before and spent a long time, almost a year, and came back. And she more or less persuaded my father that they should go to Brazil.

speaker: interviewer
text: Although my father did all his exams. He studied Hebrew, and he did all the exams as a lawyer in Israel, and he became an Israeli lawyer. And had even his own practice. I think he-- I think that the thing is going out. He's [INAUDIBLE]? No. OK. So he got your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it's flashing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop now.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's flashing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you go back a little bit?
